title: Country Sourdough Boule
intro: A blistered, mahogany crust gives way to an open, custardy crumb with a gentle tang. This naturally leavened loaf rewards patience with the finest bread you can bake at home.
ingredients: 200 g active sourdough starter (bubbly and doubled)
600 g plus 50 g warm water, divided
Rice flour, for dusting the banneton
steps: Whisk the starter into 600 g warm water, then add both flours and mix into a shaggy dough, letting it rest covered for 40 minutes to autolyse.
Add the salt with the remaining 50 g water and squeeze it through the dough until fully incorporated and smooth.
Over the next 3 hours, perform a set of stretch-and-folds every 30 minutes, keeping the dough covered and warm between turns.
Let the dough bulk ferment until it grows about 40 percent in volume and feels airy and jiggly, roughly 2 more hours.
Turn the dough out, pre-shape into a round, rest 20 minutes, then shape tightly and place seam-up in a rice-floured banneton.
Cover and cold proof in the refrigerator for 12 to 16 hours to build flavor and structure.
Preheat a Dutch oven at 500 F for 45 minutes, then invert the loaf in, score deeply, and bake covered for 20 minutes.
Uncover, lower to 460 F, and bake 25 to 30 minutes until deeply browned, then cool completely on a rack before slicing.
tip: Judge bulk fermentation by dough behavior, not the clock, since a warm kitchen speeds it up dramatically.

title: No-Knead Artisan Bread
intro: A crackly, bakery-style loaf that needs no kneading and almost no effort. Time and a hot pot do all the work while you go about your day.
ingredients: Wheat bran or cornmeal, for dusting
steps: Stir the flour, salt, and yeast together, then add the water and mix just until no dry flour remains and a sticky, shaggy dough forms.
Cover the bowl and let it rise at room temperature for 12 to 18 hours, until the surface is dotted with bubbles.
Scrape the dough onto a floured surface, fold it over on itself twice, and let it rest 15 minutes seam-side down.
Shape into a loose ball, set it on a bran-dusted towel, cover, and proof for about 2 hours until nearly doubled.
Preheat a covered Dutch oven at 475 F for 30 minutes during the final proof.
Tip the dough seam-side up into the hot pot, cover, and bake for 30 minutes.
Remove the lid and bake 12 to 18 minutes more until the crust is deep golden brown.
Cool on a rack for at least an hour so the crumb can set before you slice.
tip: A wetter dough yields a more open crumb, so resist adding extra flour when it feels sticky.

title: Rosemary Sea Salt Focaccia
intro: Dimpled, olive-oil drenched, and golden at the edges, this focaccia is pillowy within and crisp beneath. Fresh rosemary and flaky salt make every square irresistible.
ingredients: 60 ml extra-virgin olive oil, plus more for the pan
steps: Combine the flour, fine salt, and yeast, then mix in the water and 30 ml olive oil until a wet, sticky dough forms.
Cover and let rise 1 hour, folding the dough over itself four times midway to build strength.
Generously oil a rimmed sheet pan, transfer the dough, and gently stretch it toward the corners.
Cover and proof for 1 to 2 hours until puffy and bubbly.
Drizzle with the remaining oil and press your fingertips all over to create deep dimples.
Scatter rosemary leaves and flaky salt across the top.
Bake at 450 F for 22 to 25 minutes until uniformly golden and crisp at the edges.
Slide onto a rack, brush with a little more oil, and cool slightly before cutting into squares.
tip: Cold-proofing the dimpled dough overnight in the fridge deepens the flavor and makes it easier to handle.

title: Classic Butter Brioche Loaf
intro: Rich with butter and eggs, this brioche bakes into a burnished, feather-light loaf. It tears into tender, golden strands perfect for toast or French toast.
steps: Mix the flour, salt, sugar, and yeast in a stand mixer, keeping the salt and yeast on opposite sides.
Add 5 eggs and the milk, then knead on medium with the dough hook for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Add the softened butter a few pieces at a time, kneading until fully absorbed and the dough is glossy, about 6 minutes.
Cover and let rise 1 hour, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight to firm up.
Divide the chilled dough into 8 balls, arrange in a buttered loaf tin, and proof 2 hours until risen above the rim.
Brush gently with beaten egg for a glossy finish.
Bake at 375 F for 30 to 35 minutes until deep golden and hollow-sounding when tapped.
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to finish cooling.
tip: Chilling the dough is essential, since cold butter keeps the enriched dough workable and prevents a greasy crumb.

title: Chewy New York Bagels
intro: Boiled then baked, these bagels have a glossy, chewy crust and a dense, satisfying interior. An overnight cold proof gives them their signature deep flavor.
ingredients: 20 g barley malt syrup, plus 1 tablespoon for the boil
1 tablespoon baking soda, for the boil
Sesame or poppy seeds, for topping
steps: Mix the flour, salt, yeast, malt syrup, and water into a stiff dough, then knead 10 minutes until smooth and firm.
Divide into 8 pieces, roll each into a ball, and let them rest 20 minutes under a towel.
Poke a hole through each ball and stretch into rings, then place on an oiled tray, cover, and refrigerate overnight.
The next day, bring a wide pot of water to a boil with the baking soda and extra malt syrup.
Boil the bagels 45 seconds per side, then lift out to drain and dip the tops in seeds.
Arrange on a parchment-lined sheet and bake at 450 F for 18 to 22 minutes until richly browned.
Rotate the pan halfway through for even color.
Cool on a rack for 20 minutes before slicing.
tip: A pinch of baking soda in the boiling water raises its alkalinity for a shinier, chewier crust.

title: Buttery English Scones
intro: Tall, tender, and golden-topped, these classic scones split cleanly for jam and clotted cream. They come together in minutes for a proper afternoon tea.
steps: Rub the cold butter into the flour and baking powder until the mixture resembles fine crumbs, then stir in the sugar.
Warm the milk with the vanilla slightly, then pour most of it in and mix quickly to a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Turn out onto a floured surface and pat gently to about 1 inch thick without overworking.
Stamp out rounds with a floured cutter, pressing straight down without twisting so they rise evenly.
Place close together on a lined tray and brush the tops with beaten egg.
Rest 15 minutes, then bake at 425 F for 10 to 12 minutes until well risen and golden.
Cool briefly on a rack.
Serve warm, split with jam and clotted cream.
tip: Twisting the cutter seals the edges and stops the scones from rising straight and tall.

title: Overnight White Bread Loaf
intro: A straightforward straight-dough loaf with a crackly crust and soft, flavorful crumb. A long, slow rise develops depth without any special equipment.
steps: Mix the flour, salt, and yeast, then add the warm water and squeeze until fully combined into a sticky dough.
Cover and perform two sets of stretch-and-folds within the first hour.
Let the dough rise at cool room temperature for 8 to 10 hours until roughly tripled.
Turn out gently, shape into a tight round, and place seam-up in a floured proofing bowl.
Proof for 1 to 2 hours until the dough springs back slowly when poked.
Preheat a Dutch oven at 475 F for 45 minutes.
Invert the loaf into the pot, cover, and bake 30 minutes, then uncover and bake 20 minutes more until deep brown.
Cool completely on a rack before cutting.
tip: Keep the dough around 78 F during its long rise for the best balance of flavor and rise.

title: Bakery-Style Banana Bread
intro: Moist, deeply banana-forward, and lightly caramelized on top, this loaf is a study in ripe fruit and brown butter. It slices beautifully and only improves the next day.
steps: Melt the butter in a saucepan and cook until it turns nutty and amber, then let it cool slightly.
Whisk the brown butter with the brown sugar, eggs, and vanilla until smooth.
Stir in the mashed bananas until well combined.
Fold in the flour, baking soda, and salt just until no dry streaks remain.
Pour the batter into a buttered and lined loaf tin and smooth the top.
Bake at 350 F for 55 to 65 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.
Tent with foil if the top browns too quickly.
Cool in the tin 15 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to finish.
tip: The blacker the bananas, the sweeter and more intense the flavor, so let them ripen fully.

title: Frozen S'mores Croissant
intro: Flaky, laminated croissant dough filled with dark chocolate and torched marshmallow for a playful twist on a campfire classic. It is an ambitious project with a spectacular payoff.
ingredients: 280 g cold unsalted butter, for laminating
steps: Mix the flour, salt, sugar, yeast, and milk into a dough, knead briefly, then chill 1 hour.
Pound the butter into a flat square, then encase it in the rolled-out dough and seal the edges.
Roll out and fold in thirds three times, chilling 45 minutes between each fold to keep the butter cold.
Roll the laminated dough thin, cut into triangles, place chopped chocolate at the base, and roll up into croissants.
Proof at warm room temperature for 2 hours until visibly puffy and jiggly.
Bake at 400 F for 16 to 20 minutes until deep golden and crisp, then cool completely.
Split each croissant, pipe in marshmallow fluff, and torch the top until toasted.
Dust with crushed graham crackers and serve at once.
tip: Keep everything cold throughout lamination, since warm butter melts into the dough and ruins the flaky layers.

title: Blueberry Streusel Muffins
intro: Domed and bursting with juicy berries under a crunchy streusel lid, these muffins are bakery-worthy. A quick batter means fresh muffins in under an hour.
ingredients: 60 g cold butter and 80 g flour for streusel
steps: Rub the cold butter into the streusel flour with a spoonful of sugar until crumbly, then set aside.
Whisk the flour, baking powder, baking soda, salt, and sugar in a large bowl.
In another bowl, whisk the eggs, oil, and yogurt until smooth.
Fold the wet ingredients into the dry just until combined, then gently stir in the blueberries.
Divide the batter among 12 lined muffin cups and scatter streusel over each.
Bake at 400 F for 5 minutes, then reduce to 375 F and bake 18 to 20 minutes more.
Test with a skewer for a clean center.
Cool in the pan 10 minutes before transferring to a rack.
tip: Tossing the blueberries in a little flour first keeps them suspended instead of sinking to the bottom.

title: Rustic Whole Wheat Pain de Campagne
intro: An earthy country loaf balancing hearty whole wheat with a light, airy crumb. Its thick crust and nutty flavor make it a versatile everyday bread.
steps: Combine the starter, both flours, and 350 g water into a rough dough and rest 30 minutes to hydrate.
Add the salt and remaining water, mixing thoroughly until incorporated.
Fold the dough every 45 minutes for the first 3 hours, keeping it covered and warm.
Continue bulk fermenting until risen by about a third and airy, roughly 2 more hours.
Shape into a tight round and place seam-up in a floured banneton, then cold proof overnight.
Preheat a Dutch oven at 480 F for 40 minutes.
Turn the loaf out, score once, bake covered 22 minutes, then uncover and bake 22 minutes more.
Cool fully on a rack before slicing to let the crumb set.
tip: Whole wheat drinks up more water, so a slightly wetter dough keeps this loaf from baking up dense.

title: Soft Cinnamon Rolls
intro: Pillowy enriched dough spiraled with cinnamon sugar and finished with a tangy glaze. These rolls fill the kitchen with an irresistible aroma as they bake.
ingredients: 50 g melted butter, plus 80 g softened for filling
120 g brown sugar and 2 tablespoons cinnamon for filling
100 g cream cheese and 100 g icing sugar for glaze
steps: Mix the flour, salt, sugar, and yeast, then add the warm milk and melted butter and knead 8 minutes until smooth.
Cover and let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Roll the dough into a large rectangle, spread with softened butter, and sprinkle evenly with brown sugar and cinnamon.
Roll up tightly from the long edge and cut into 12 equal slices.
Arrange in a buttered baking dish, cover, and proof 45 minutes until puffy and touching.
Bake at 375 F for 22 to 25 minutes until golden and cooked through.
Beat the cream cheese with icing sugar and a splash of milk into a smooth glaze.
Spread the glaze over the warm rolls and serve.
tip: Cut the rolls with unflavored dental floss to get clean slices without squashing the spiral.
